speaker: interviewee
text: --while I have my tea. I mean, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Phyllis Dreazen, interviewer, interviewing Helen Zelinger nee Rynski. It's June 9, 1995, and we are in Northbrook, Illinois. What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Helen Zelinger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Zelinger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-E-L-I-N-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rynski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-Y-N-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names, nicknames, Hebrew names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My Hebrew name was Chana, and my middle name was Tova.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell those?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can spell Tova. It's the Hebrew [HEBREW].

speaker: interviewer
text: Or in English, T-O-V-A.

speaker: interviewee
text: A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is 1925, August 8.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 70-- I'm 69, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland. This city was called Szczekociny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-Z-C-Z-E-K-O-C-I-N-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that near?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was near Sosnowiec, Kielce, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And which was the biggest city of those?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to try spelling it?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-O-S-N-O-W-I-E-C.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your town, would you describe it as far as size and look and modernity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's-- before the war, I was a very young child. My parents did financially very well. My father had a small brewery, had a quite lovely Jewish community. I know of the girlfriends that I have and there were boys that we were very close. There were all kinds of people. There were people-- business people. There were doctors. There were engineers. There were quite a few families that they were prosperous and sent to other cities their children to high school because we did not have a high school for higher education. And I did-- I did have a good life till the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large a Jewish community was in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really cannot tell you that. I have no way of knowing or remembering.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Jewish community well integrated or was-- did they separate themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Integrated. Very much so. It wasn't just one spot where all the Jews lived. Because where we lived, I mean, I remember just very distinctly now, we lived across a church, a big apartment. And we had Jewish people and Gentile people. And at the time, also my father being in those business, which basically the Gentile people bought-- used to buy the beer, smaller towns, I mean little around Szczekociny, and they were very friendly and courteous to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a way. My father comes from a very religious home. My mother comes from a traditional home. And we had a tradition in the house. My father used to go to the temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: When would he go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically on a Saturday or Friday evening. Friday evening or Saturday, not holidays, but not during the week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he work on Friday or Saturday?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. You know, we employed like 25 people, so they were in charge for the weekends-- not just Friday and Saturday but also Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, did she cover her hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, as I understand, wore a wig for a few years when she got married. But my days, I don't remember her, and she did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they both from that town? Were they born in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both from the same town, yes. It's the only thing that after the First World War, my mother's family left Szczekociny for Sosnowiec, which this was also a ride with a bus, two hours, an hour and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot tell you. I really never asked my mother and she never told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they an arranged marriage?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not that I know. I think he fell in love. He met her someplace. That's what she told me. She had the most gorgeous legs, and he always used to admire that. That's my dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she still does, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: She still does. For a lady of 90 years old, she really looks great. Her legs are prettier than mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your legs are pretty good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Henry.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry was born in May 29, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does he live now?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in New Orleans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, is he alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father passed away 17 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Milwaukee. They lived in Milwaukee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else live with you in your home before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you describe your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a three-- three rooms apartment, which one room was the kitchen, and there was a dining room and a bathroom. I do remember-- I mean, we did not have gas or electricity. We had an oven that was warm. I mean, we can prepare just with coals. Besides this, in the winter, you used to have a special-- it looked like a barrel, but it was made from aluminum, and you used to fill this up with sawdust. It was round and in the middle was a pipe. So this sawdust was all around and when you lit up the bottom, you took out the pipe, so this way the saw dust could warm the house during the winter nights. We were very comfortable. As a child, I don't remember to being denied of anything-- vacations, going to resorts with my parents, with my brother, with the family. I was very close with my mother's family, with all the uncles and aunts. They loved me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were nine children, including my mother. And they hardly could wait on a vacation that I should come to visit to them, and I really admired my grandmother. That's my mother's mother. Her name was Ziesl. I have an aunt who was nine months older than I am, but I was such a devil that before she could go to my grandma, I was on her lap already. And there was a rivalry. She really didn't like me those days because whatever she wanted to do, I was ahead of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name, this aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: She survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: She survived in Russia. She lives in Milwaukee and has a family of two daughters. And now she has five grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Does she like you better now?

speaker: interviewee
text: She doesn't like me better, but I tell you a little story. My grandma had her when Grandma was 47 years old. That was the last baby because she got the birth certificate from Poland. I remember her, playing with her and her girlfriends in Sosnowiec, but she had always the fear coming home and seeing her mother dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reason? All her girlfriends had much younger mothers and fathers. And this she shared with me after we found her after the war, that all her life she had anxiety. She was very close to her mother. As a matter of fact, they were in Russia and she buried them. They had the typhus. So she went through hardship in her life also, but now we get along beautifully. We always did. If my mother made the dress for me, in Europe, used to be not that you go to a department store and you pick your size. You had to have a seamstress, and she would make you clothes. My mother never left her out. Whatever she did for me, she did for her. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewee
text: And now she's well, we see each other, and we speak of the years previous, pleasant memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to school there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go to school with my aunt. I went to school in Szczekociny, but in 1939, before the war broke out, I was supposed to start high school, and that was in Sosnowiec. But unfortunate, the war was there, and the doors were closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the primary school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The primary school was my years, very enjoyable. We had-- I would say in each class, we had maybe 45% Jewish kids. The rest were Gentile. We had a Jewish teacher who taught us religion. The only thing which we had to do when the Christian children had their class, we used to leave the class and when we had once a week, ours, they used to leave. But beautiful memories about the teachers. The Jewish lady was just beautiful and we had only one man that I remember his name. Kacamarczek was the teacher from Polish. And I spoke a beautiful Polish, and he admired me for that. We had difficult times Saturdays because we didn't go to school, but the Gentile children did. So on a Sunday or Saturday evening after school, if we came over to their homes to find out what they were taught or what the assignment, most of the time, they resented it. So that was a difficult time in school, but the rest were very pleasant memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your friends Jewish and non-Jewish both?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the names of your special friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have a very special friend that she lives now in Israel and her name was just like me, but she is Hanka. And if you want me to spell, it's H-A-N-K-A. She survived also in Russia with her sister and a brother. They live in Israel. Each time I go there, we have a beautiful reunion, but her parents passed away, her brother passed away of cancer. And three years ago, when we were in Israel, she looked very sick, but she did not admit that she has cancer, and she passed away. So this is my dearest friend that survived from the whole Jewish-- I mean, friends from Szczekociny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside of school, what would you do to play or to have fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my hobbies were ice skating, if I call it a hobby. I used to love it. I started at a very young age. I used to enjoy the environment from boyfriends also from school, and we used to go on a kayak. I'd ride two people in it, and that used to be always on a Saturday afternoon. And to be honest, I never told this my mother because she really wouldn't like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it's the boys, and it's the water. And I did have once an experience that it really turned over, and I was not a swimmer. And that gave me a lot of anxiety that I never swam since then. But at my old age, when they said swimming is very good, I taught myself and now I swim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do kayaks after that or other boats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did it because we put something, you know. But basically that was, you know. I have to mention that I went to religious school. That's Tarbut, which this took also three hours of the day every day. I knew how to read the Torah. I knew how to read [HEBREW], [HEBREW]. We had a lot-- little place prepared the Hebrew that our parents used to come and enjoy, and this took a whole day of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say you were more religious or more rebellious?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not religious. It was a tradition. In my home, basically it was a tradition.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where you Zionistic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not me. No. My mother did, belonged to WZO, that the Zionist Organization, she was very much involved in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The World Zionist Organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did she do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, what it was is, I mean, making bazaars where you sell stuff and you make the money, bakery sales, fashion shows if you call it in Poland. You know, if you made yourself a dress or the seamstress and they called, you came and showed off your new clothes. This was a raise fund, and this went towards Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your traditional home, do you have any special memories of holidays or foods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much. Very much so. Very much so. Especially my grandma in Sosnowiec. We used to go to her every Passover. And if I remember correctly, we used to take-- on the top of the bus, there were live chickens. There were eggs. My mother used to spend three nights at a Jewish bakery where they make all the pastries and everything was shipped with my family, which it means my father, my mom, my brother. We went for the whole week to Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: For Passover.

speaker: interviewee
text: For Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: With matzahs.

speaker: interviewee
text: With matzahs, that was grandma's already part. She did it, but it was experience. As a child, I remember she covered the tablecloths with special-- the tables with special cloths. Everything was washed, which my mother did not. She had separate dishes, and I was very impressed with that. And at the Seder nacht, we were about 25 people, which my grandfather, [? Motel, ?] was sitting in a white-- I don't know what they call it. It's not a robe.

speaker: interviewer
text: The kittel.

speaker: interviewee
text: The kittel, the white kittel-- not just sitting, but leaning. And we all were just so hypnotized by the things what were going on. And no movement and no speaking back. You sat like this till 1 o'clock in the morning. But we enjoyed every moment of it. And at the end, we made the journey back home. So this is the best I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What special foods did she make?

speaker: interviewee
text: She made special thing that I enjoyed, not for Passover, was a Easter cake with chocolate. And when I used to come visit, she used to hide it because she knew it's going to be gone in an hour. So she gave me every day another extra piece, and I loved it. Otherwise, I don't remember anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: The tablecloths, the Passover dishes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was young. I mean, as I say, I was impressed with special different dishes that you never used during-- if there was no holiday. I was impressed with the centerpiece. I was impressed the way all the uncles-- they were younger then-- were dressed in the little suits with the ties and with us made special new clothes. If it starts with the dress to new shoes. And you look forwards for this time of the year because you knew you were always getting something new.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about for Rosh Hashanah? The new year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosh Hashanah, we used to spend at home because of the traveling and Yom Kippur is so close. So we used to go to Grandma Ziesl, to the parents of my father's and that was a completely different situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that-- you couldn't move by grandma. I mean, you couldn't ask for anything. She put on the table, and you could eat. You had a choice, yes or no. I used to [INAUDIBLE], but she was very strict. I couldn't come with her with a short sleeve. I couldn't come to her visit with no stockings on.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was very religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very religious and I resented this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I wanted comfort as a child. The Tarbut, the religious school that I went, we came. We could go with no hose, we could go with short sleeves, and that's what I wanted to do. So she really wasn't pleased with me because I knew when I have to go and visit, I have to change. So I try to avoid this as much as I could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you successful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your avoidance techniques?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe there was not a technique. She knew it, what the problem is, so she didn't ask as much. And I was happy about it, and I didn't go as much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get new clothes for new year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my mother basically made clothes all-- you know, it wasn't just new year. She made basically-- we had a seamstress. I remember very well, her, because I was never good with it. I could never stand still or listen to her. But we used to go quite a few times during the year because she had so many customers, and she couldn't produce as much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember. Sweet lady, but I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would she get you to stand during the fitting?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think maybe I don't remember it was a piece of chocolate, it was a cookie, or maybe a hard candy. And any child can be thrilled with this, so I guess I was also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Among these boys in your life, did you have a special boyfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was David. He still is alive. He is in Israel. Comes from a very fine family. He was a few years older, so when I finished school, he was already in Sosnowiec in high school. I didn't see him since-- I didn't see him during the war. But after the war when the first time we went to Israel in 1964, I met quite a few survivors, and he was one of them also. But he was married at the time, which I was delighted, and he was the only survivor from his family. His sister was a very good friend of mine. That's why I befriended myself with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you when you took him for a boyfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would explain that the boyfriend is not to that extreme the way it's in the United States. You used to go to school, maybe you went out skating once with him, but it wasn't hanging out to a movie go or hanging out for ice cream. It was just at-- in the school. It made you feel he's there. I cannot express my feelings now as was a young kid how you felt towards-- but they knew he was my boyfriend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that give you special status?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it give him special status?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea because I was very young and we never went in talking to each other, I love you, I miss you, whatever, never happened. No, just a school friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this happy childhood life, what was the first sense that something might be wrong? Was there any-- the first anti-Semitic thing that you became aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: At my own hometown, I wasn't aware of anything. But where my grandparents lived in Sosnowiec, there used to be a main street called [POLISH]-- this street in Polish. One sight at my childhood-- which maybe when I was 11, 12 years old, there was one street the Jews walked and one street the Gentiles walked. I remember my grandfather once coming home in the evening when I was visiting, he was beat up by three Gentile boys, and that's all my memory because I didn't live there. I just came that time visiting, and I just heard what they used to tell. And if I went to this-- I mean to the [POLISH] with my aunt, the younger one, Sarah, and the girlfriends, we used to walk on the side where the Jewish people were suppose to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would Gentiles be beaten up if they walked on the Jewish side?

speaker: interviewee
text: You wouldn't want to do it because you took a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware by listening to the radio that war was coming or Hitler was getting more--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Very much so because there was announcement from the government, you know, for the night to cover up the windows, seal them up so they won't see in the light. And that they gave us warning of preparing in the house. Go to the grocery, buy enough flour, sugar, bread so you have at least for a week. I'm very much aware of it. And, and that was very quick. It's a week. My uncle, Mendel, who lives now in Australia actually came from Sosnowiec to us because he thought it's going to be a safer place. And the 3rd one of September, the early morning, my father woke up and he said, kids, get dressed and it's time to go to a smaller town. We will stay overnight, and we come back because the Germans are beginning to bombard.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: '39, September 3. He said, dress yourself, take what you can, and we go. And at the time, it was such a panic that my mother grabbed a few rolls from the refrigerator, whatever we had. I put on my uniform that I used to go to school because I came from school and in the morning usually you prepare the night before. We used to wear black-- what-- black uniforms with a white little collar. And that's what I run away from home. My mother in her dress with a few rolls, my brother, my dad. No money, no jewelry, no clothes, nothing. And we never saw our home again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the little town you were supposed to go, where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was maybe 20 miles away from us, but we heard what's going on on the radio that the Germans are coming closer, and my father decide we just going to run so far. Eventually the Germans won't take over all Poland. We will wait till it's over, and then we return home. But there was no-- and there was no really plan where we going because we got short of gas. Gas was not available to buy. They only saved for the military.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got in the car, and you went how far before it conked out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know the day, the mileage, but I remember our first stop. First stops used to be small towns, but the big town, we stopped in Lublin. And I tell you a little story about it that I heard from my father. There was the prime minister of Poland. I don't remember his name. He came there with the [NON-ENGLISH] to sleep over that night. They were running also.
